Okay, so now that we've talked about how to promote and how to not get promoted, let's talk about common mistakes that are made. Okay, and so I want to first talk about mistakes that the management makes, mistakes that the CEO makes, mistakes that the business owner makes, right? One is lack of separation, okay? So what you need to do is you need to make sure that you're always separating the person's new job from their old job, okay? And in that transition time, if you let them overlap too long, the person is doing both jobs. And when that happens, they're not going to succeed in either one. And so they're not given a fair opportunity to even step into the next role, right? So it's like what I talked about with Delegate to Elevate. Like if you want to give more responsibility to someone, you also need to relieve them in some way, otherwise they can't do both, right? You're just splitting their attention way too much. Second is that they have a training deficit, okay? So the current teammate or supervisor feels that he or she can train the new person, but then becomes busy with other business demands, and the person doesn't even go through a full set of training, okay? I have had this happen to me early on, like two years ago. I remember I didn't realize that we had a trip the next week. I brought someone on and I let me tell you, she came on and I was gone for eight days when I could have been and should have been onboarding her. And she had a terrible representation of the business. Like her, when she came in, she was so unsure. She felt like she had no tools to succeed. And ultimately, like we end up having to let her go because she just, she couldn't perform. And I tried to re-onboarding her and I'm just telling you, it's a first impression. You have to make sure that you have the time set aside to make sure that you do not, you're not the cause of any of their deficits when it comes to training, okay? Third, keeping it a secret, okay? Some people do not announce positions. They do not let their members know. They don't let their team know. They don't make a big deal about it. And let me tell you, you need to promote internally. You need to invite in this person. You need to do a shout out in daily huddle. You need to send email to the team. You need to let the clients know, okay? Because it's just like, it's celebrating someone. It's welcoming them in. And if you do it big and you break it, like it's a huge deal that you brought this person on, they're going to step up a lot faster than if you were like, keep it a secret, it's a little thing, because then they're not going to feel important, right? And you want everyone to know how valued they are. Fourth is time to hire, okay? So a lot of people hire internally out of desperation, especially for a management position. Happens, I'm pretty sure every business owner has done this. It's like they need someone really fast. And so they promote someone who's interested from within and that person was not ready for the job. And they didn't have the skills to do it, right? And so don't let urgency create bad decisions. Lastly is people pleasing, okay? So a lot of managers try to appease the workers who have worked for them the longest by offering them a promotion or a transfer or whatever within the company, even though it's not the best decision for the business, it's the best they think it's doing right by the teammate. Reality is the best decision for the business is the best decision for the teammate. And so if that means that they want a position that they're not a fit for in this company, but they're so set on having that position, then maybe they have to go get it at a different company. Like that's the reality of life. And so do not promote people and because you want to make them happy or you feel indebted, that is the worst thing you can do for your business and for them. Mistakes employees make when it comes to promotions, okay? One, when they are trying to get a promotion, they ask for too much, right? A lot of times what happens is someone asks for a promotion, a raise and more freedom all at the same time, right? And so this is going to super frustrate your manager, supervisor, whatever. You want to make sure that you isolate it. Ask for one, not the others or ask how. If you don't know how, like how can I get promoted? How could I get a raise? You know, how can I get more freedom? Pick one and go down on that and the rest will come in time, but you're going to overwhelm your manager if you bring all those at the same time, because they're kind of contingent on doing one well. Second is overshooting your target, right? And so a lot of employees make the mistake of asking for a promotion. They don't yet have the skills to learn, right? And if you're not sure if you have the skill set, what makes more sense is to have the discussion with your manager or supervisor about, hey, you know, I wanted to know what I need to work on to be considered for this position, right? Rather than saying I deserve an interview because like I'm freaking crushing it here just because you crushed it here, doesn't mean you're gonna crush it over here. That's the reality. You have to be able to look at yourself objectively. It's called self-awareness. Third, choosing status over fulfillment. Okay? Man, people do this all the time. Employees think that they want the next thing simply because they want status. They want a raise. They lack objectivity to look at their strengths versus their weaknesses and look at the strengths of the role versus their natural strengths and then decide if it's a good decision for them. Most people, they just want more authority. They want more freedom. They want more money and they want more status and that's they're gonna do whatever they have to do to get that and then when they get there, they're freaking miserable. Okay? So it's up to you to sit there and look at like, hey, I don't think that you would be happy doing that and here's why and giving them a very clear idea of what the role actually entails. Right? Fourth, not telling. Okay? So a lot of teammates don't tell someone and don't raise their hand and say, hey, I want a promotion or hey, I want to know what I have to do for a promotion. Right? Because they're shy or they're introverted or they're nervous or uncomfortable. And actually, it's introverts that usually fall easily into this trap and are really the ones who need to step out of their comfort zones. But the thing is, is that if you don't voice that you want growth in a company, if you don't voice that you want a promotion, you will never get one and you shouldn't expect one. It is not just your manager's job to ask you. It is your job to push and tell because they don't know when the light bulb goes off in your brain and suddenly you want one. Right? If you've said no the last three months, they might be like, all right, well, they really don't want it. And it's up to you to bring that forward to them and tell them. Right? Two-way communication. And then last is stepping on others. Of course, it is obvious that most promotions are due to an increase in responsibility because someone has done one job with excellence. However, it doesn't really matter how great the results are if people don't trust you or they have a time hard time working with you. Right? And so I've seen a ton of high performers who they can get the results and get the job done, but it leads the entire team hating them because they stepped on everyone's toes. They went behind other people's backs. They basically micro-managed an entire business to get something done for themselves. Right? And so another thing is do not do that in order to get promoted. Oftentimes people with will expect a promotion due to their results individually rather than their contribution as a team. But people who get promoted, it's typically because they're being put in a leadership positions, are the highest team contributors, not self-contributors. Right?
